title: Beyond Object Recognition: A New Benchmark towards Object Concept Learning

paragraphs.text:
Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## - BEYOND OBJECT RECOGNITION: A NEW BENCH MARK TOWARDS OBJECT CONCEPT LEARNING

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

Understanding objects is a central building block of artificial intelligence, especially for embodied AI. Even though object recognition excels with deep learning, current machines still struggle to learn higher-level knowledge, e.g., what attributes does an object have, what can we do with an object. In this work, we propose a challenging **Object Concept Learning** (OCL) task to push the envelope of object understanding. It requires machines to reason out object affordances and simultaneously give the reason: _what attributes make an object possesses these affordances_ . To support OCL, we build a _densely_ annotated knowledge base including extensive labels for three levels of object concept: categories, attributes, and affordances, and their causal relations. By analyzing the causal structure of OCL, we present a baseline, Object Concept Reasoning Network (OCRN). It leverages causal intervention and concept instantiation to infer the three levels following their causal relations. In experiments, OCRN effectively infers the object knowledge while follows the causalities well. Data and code will be publicly available.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Object understanding is essential for intelligent machines. Recently, benefited by deep learning and large-scale datasets (Deng et al., 2009; Lin et al., 2014), category recognition (Krizhevsky et al., 2012; Ren et al., 2015) has made tremendous leaps of progress. But to close the gap between human and machine perception, machines need to pursue deeper object understanding, e.g., recognizing higher-level attributes (Isola et al., 2015) and affordances (Gibson, 2014), which may help them establish the concept of objects (Martin, 2007) when interacting with complex contexts.

Category apple is a symbol indicating its referent (real apple). In line with symbol grounding (Harnad, 1990), machines should learn knowledge beyond category to approach concept understanding. According to cognition studies (Ross, 2008; Martin, 2007), attributes depicting objects from the physical/visual side play an important role in object understanding. Thus, many works (Lampert et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Farhadi et al., 2009) studied to ground objects with attributes, e.g., a hammer consists of a long handle and a heavy head. Moreover, attributes can depict object _state_ (Isola et al., 2015). An elegant characteristic of attributes is cross-category: objects of the same category can have various states (big or fresh apple), whilst various objects can have the same state (sliced orange or carrot). If the category is the **first** level of object concept, the attribute can be seen as the **second** level closer to the physical fact.

However, recognizing attributes is still far away from concept understanding. Given a hammer, we need to know it can be held to hit nails. That is, requiring machines to infer affordance (Gibson, 2014) indicating what actions humans can perform upon objects. Here, we refer to affordance as the **third** level, which is closely related to common sense and causal inference (Gibson, 2014). Although affordance learning has been studied in robotics (Do et al., 2018; Hermans et al., 2011) and vision (Chuang et al., 2018; Zhu et al., 2014) communities in decades, it is still challenging. First, previous works (Nguyen et al., 2017; Fouhey et al., 2015) often focus on recognizing affordance solely. But we usually infer affordance based on attribute observation. When we need to knock in a nail without a hammer at hand, we may find other hard or heavy objects instead, e.g., a thick book. This profoundly reveals the **causality** between attribute and affordance. Second, previous works are designed for category/scale/scene-limited tasks, e.g., in Zhu et al. (2014), 40 objects and 14 affordances are included; Hermans et al. (2011) collect 375 indoor images of 6 objects, 21

To reshape object learning, we believe it is essential to look at the above three levels in a **unified** and **causal** way based on an extensive knowledge base. Hence, we move a step forward to propose the object concept learning (OCL) task: given an object, machines need to infer its category, attributes, and further answer “ _what can we do upon it and why_ ”. In a nutshell, machines need to reason affordance based on object appearance, category, and attributes. To this end, we build a largescale and dense dataset consisting of **381** object categories, **114** attributes, and **170** affordances. It contains **80,463** images of diverse scenes, **185,941** object instances in different states. Different from previous works (Chao et al., 2015; Hermans et al., 2011; Zhu et al., 2014), OCL offers a more nuanced angle. It includes: (1) **category** -level attribute ( _A_ ) and affordance ( _B_ ) labels; (2) **instance** - level attribute ( _α_ ) and affordance ( _β_ ) labels. Besides, we consider and annotate the _causal relations_ between three levels to evaluate the reasoning ability of models and keep the follow-up methods from fitting data only. Accordingly, based on the causal structure of OCL, we also propose a _neurocausal_ method, **O** bject **C** oncept **R** easoning **N** etwork ( **OCRN** ), as the future baseline. It operates the _instantiation_ from category-level to instance-level, and leverages _causal intervention_ (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the bias from categories and infer attributes and affordances. OCRN outperforms a host of baselines and shows impressive performance while follows the causal relations well.

In summary, our contributions are threefold: (1) Introducing the object concept learning (OCL) task poses challenges and opportunities for deeper object understanding and knowledge-based reasoning. (2) Building a dataset consists of diverse objects, elaborate attributes and affordances, together with their causal relations to benchmark OCL. (3) An object concept reasoning network (OCRN) is introduced to reason three levels with concept instantiation and debiasing, which performs well.

## 2 RELATED WORK

**Object Attribute.** Attribute depicts the visual/physical properties like color, size, shape, etc. It usually plays the role of intermedia between pixels and higher-level concepts, e.g., prompting object recognition (Farhadi et al., 2009), affordance learning (Hermans et al., 2011), zero-shot learning (Lampert et al., 2009), and object detection (Kumar Singh et al., 2018). Recently, several largescale datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Liu et al., 2017; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Isola et al., 2015; Krishna et al., 2016; Hudson & Manning, 2019) are released. For attribute recognition, recent works have made progress. Beside direct multi-label classification (Lampert et al., 2009; Parikh & Grauman, 2011; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016) or leveraging the correlation between attribute-attribute and attribute-object (Hwang et al., 2011; Chen & Grauman, 2014; Mahajan et al., 2011), intrinsic properties (compositionality, contextuality (Misra et al., 2017; Nagarajan & Grauman, 2018), symmetry (Li et al., 2020b)) of attribute-object are also proven useful.

**Object Affordance.** The concept of affordance is introduced by Gibson (2014). Affordance learning has two canonical paradigms: direct mapping (Fouhey et al., 2015) or indirect method (Zhu et al., 2014; Zhao & Zhu, 2013; Wang et al., 2017; Roy & Todorovic, 2016) with intermedia like object category, attribute, and 3D contents. Some studies also tried to learn affordance from human-object interactions, i.e., encoding the relationship between object and action (Gupta & Davis, 2007; Yao et al., 2013; Kato et al., 2018). Visual Genome (Krishna et al., 2016) provides visual relationships between objects in the scene graph, including some human actions instead of affordances. However, these relationships cover limited and sparse affordances. Differently, we use easily accessible object images as the knowledge source, and _densely_ annotate all attributes/affordances for all object instances. Besides the vision community, the robot community also pays much attention to affordance (Do et al., 2018; Pinto & Gupta, 2016; Thermos et al., 2017; Pinto et al., 2016), especially for

(2014) built a knowledge base containing attribute, affordance, human pose, and human-object spatial configuration. But labeling pose and human-object relative location are costly. Chao et al. (2015) proposed a _semantic_ category-level affordance dataset including 91 COCO objects (Lin et al., 2014) and 957 affordances.

**Causal Inference.** There is increasing literature on exploiting causal inference (Pearl et al., 2016) in machine learning, especially with causal graphical models (Spirtes et al., 2000; Pearl et al., 2016), including feature selection (Guyon et al., 2007) and learning (Chalupka et al., 2014), video analysis (Pickup et al., 2014; Lebeda et al., 2015), reinforcement learning (Nair et al., 2019; Dasgupta et al., 2019), etc. Recently, Wang et al. (2020a) studied the causal relation between objects in images and used intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the observation bias in detection. Atzmon et al. (2020) analyze the causal generative model of compositional zero-shot learning and disentangle the representations of attributes and objects. In this work, we explore the causal relations between three object levels and apply backdoor adjustment (Pearl et al., 2016) to alleviate the existing bias.

## 3 KNOWLEDGE BASE CONSTRUCTION

Our goal is to construct a dataset that can characterize abundant knowledge in object category, attribute, affordance, and causal relations. The construction process is given as follows (Fig. 2).

**Data Collection. (1) Affordance** : We collect 170 affordances out of 1,006 candidates from widelyused action/affordance datasets (Chao et al., 2015; Gu et al., 2018; Chao et al., 2018; Gupta & Malik, 2015; Zhu et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2017) in view of generality and commonness. **(2) Category** : Considering the taxonomy (WordNet (Fellbaum, 2012)) and diversity, we collect 381 objects out of 1,742 candidates from object datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Lin et al., 2014). **(3) Attribute** : When viewed through the lens of the causal relation between attribute-affordance, we manually filter 500 most frequent attributes from large-scale attribute datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2016) and choose 114 attributes, covering colors, deformations, supercategories, various surface, geometrical, and physical properties. **(4) Image** : We extract 75,578 images from object datasets (Farhadi et al., 2009; Xiao et al., 2010; Patterson & Hays, 2016; Liu et al., 2017; Krishna et al., 2016), together with their Ground Truth (GT) boxes. To ensure diversity, we further manually collected 4,885 Internet images of selected categories. Then, we annotate the missing box and category labels for all instances via crowdsourcing. Finally, **185,941** instances of **381** categories from **80,463** images are collected, i.e., average 488 instances per category and 2.31 boxes per image. More details and class lists are in _Appendix Sec. A.11_ . OCL is long-tail distributed, where the head categories have over 5,000 instances each, but the rarest categories have only 9 instances, which challenges the robustness of vision systems.

**Attribute Annotation.** We offer annotations in two levels: (1) **Category-level attribute** ( _A_ ) contains common sense. For each _category_ , we annotate its **most common** attributes. In concept learning, the usage of category-level annotation as common knowledge can date back to Osherson et al. (1991). Following Osherson et al. (1991), to avoid bias, annotators are given _category-attribute_

(2) **Instance-level attribute** ( _α_ ) is the individual attributes of **each instance** . The annotation unit is an _attribute-instance pair_ . Each pair is labeled by multi-annotator.

**Affordance Annotation.** There are also two levels of granularity: (1) **Category-level affordance** _B_ , similar to _A_ , is annotated in _category-affordance pairs_ , indicating the common affordances of each category. Following Chao et al. (2015), the annotators label category-level affordance matrix _MB_ ([381 _,_ 170]) similar to _A_ annotation. (2) **Instance-level affordance** _β_ is annotated for **every instance** with the help of _object states_ (Isola et al., 2015). As _B_ is determined by common states, objects in specific states may have different affordances from _B_ , e.g., we cannot board a flying plane. Moreover, if a service robot finds a broken cup, it may infer that the cup can still hold water as it is trained with _B_ labels. Thus, we need detailed _β_ beyond _B_ . As the instances in the same state should have similar _β_ (all rotten apples cannot be eaten), six experts first conclude the states. In total, **1,376** states are defined, and each category has 3.6 states on average. Next, _β_ is annotated for each state and the instances are assigned with _beta_ according to their states. More details please refer to _Appendix Sec. A.2_ It is worth noting that, as states seem to be a beneficial latent variable for OCL, in practice, we do not use it in models because the states we use are a _subset_ of whole state space, and there can be _unseen_ states in real-world data.

**Causal Relation Annotation.** We annotate _instance_ -level (considering the context of each instance) causality to answer _which attribute(s) are the critical and direct causes of a certain affordance?_ **(1) Filtering** to avoid the combinatorial explosion. Initially, we need to make binary decisions on all _instance-α-β_ triplets, which is far beyond handleable. Fortunately, we find that **most** _α_ - _β_ classes (e.g., shiny and kick) are meaningless and always of no causality on any instances. Thus, we can firstly exclude the most impossible pairs, meanwhile, guarantee the completeness of causality. Finally, we obtain about 10% _α_ - _β_ classes as candidates. **(2) Instance-level causality** : we also adopt object states as a reference. Multiple annotators have been involved for each _state-α-β_ triplet and are asked whether the specific attribute is the _direct_ cause of this affordance in this state. The answers are combined and checked for all instances of a state. Finally, we obtain about 2 M _instance-α-β_ triplets of causal relations. As we have labelled all _α_ and _β_ for all instances, the causal relations would be in four situations: [0,0], [1,1]; [0,1], [1,0]. The former two are “positive”, e.g., fresh(1/0) _→_ eat(1/0) for an apple. While the last two are “negative”, e.g., broken(1/0) _→_ drive(0/1) for a _car_ .

**Discussion.** Fig. 3 shows some examples of OCL. These clear causalities are not thoroughly studied in previous datasets (Zhu et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2017; Fouhey et al., 2015; Hermans et al., 2011). We compare OCL with previous dense datasets in Tab. 1. More analysis figures and tables are in _Appendix Sec. A.3_ .

## 4 OBJECT CONCEPT LEARNING

## 4.1 TASK OVERVIEW
Given an instance _I_ (image region in box _bo_ ), OCL aims to infer attribute _α_ and affordance _β_ while following the causalities. Formally, OCL can be described as:

_< Pα, Pβ >_ = _F_ ( _I, P_ ( _O|I_ )) _,_ ∆ _Pβ_ = _TDE_ [ _F_ ( _I, P_ ( _O|I_ ))] _,_ (1)

where _Pα, Pβ_ are the probabilities of _α, β_ , given an object instance _I_ and its predicted category probability _P_ ( _O|I_ ). ∆ _Pβ_ means the Total Direct Effect ( _TDE_ [ _·_ ]) (Pearl et al., 2016) of affordance prediction change after we operate upon a model _F_ ( _·_ ). ∆ _Pβ_ is expected to follow the GT causal relation between attribute-affordance. We will detail the reasoning evaluation in Sec. 5.

We spilt images into train, validation (val) and test sets with 56,916:14,446:9,101 images. The val and test sets cover 221 of the 381 categories, and the train set covers all categories. OCL is a long-tailed recognition task and requires generalization to cover the whole object category-attributeaffordance space with imbalanced information. Thus, it is challenging for current vision systems without the reasoning ability to understand the causalities.

## 4.2 OBJECT CONCEPT REASONING NETWORK

Here, we first analyze the causal structure of OCL and then propose a basic system, Object Concept Reasoning Network (OCRN), as the future baseline for the OCL task.

## 4.2.1 ATTRIBUTE ESTIMATION

To implement Eq. 3, we use _mean_ object category feature to represent category-level attribute _A_ . We take the mean feature ( _f_[¯] _Oi_ ) of all instances in one category as the representation of the category ( _Oi_ ). It is then mapped to attribute feature space: _fAi_ = _FA_ ( _f_[¯] _Oi_ ), where _FA_ ( _·_ ) is a fully-connected layer (FC) (Fig. 5). The representation set of _A_ is _fA_ = _{fAi}[m] i_ =1[.][For each category, we use] _[ f][I]_[of] its all instances (train set) to calculate _f_[¯] _Oi_ in training and inference.

**Attribute Instantiation.** Next, we obtain _α_ representation according to Eq. 3:

where _POi_ is the _prior_ category probability ( _P_ ( _Oi_ ) in Eq. 3). Eq. 4 indicates the attribute _instantiation_ from _A_ to _α_ with _I_ as the _condition_ . Hence, we can equally translate the _α_ estimation problem into a **conditioned instantiation problem** . _Fα_ ( _·_ ) is implemented with multi-head attention mechanism (Vaswani et al., 2017), as shown in Fig. 5. Both _fAi_ and _fI_ are fed to three independent linear transformations to obtain key, query, and value vectors _K, Q, V_ and _K[′] , Q[′] , V[′]_ . **Ablation Study** . We conduct ablation studies to verify the components of OCRN w/ _LT DE_ (Tab. 3) on the val set. **(1) Deconfounding.** OCRN w/o deconfounding is implemented following Eq. 2 and 5, where _P_ ( _O|I_ ) and _P_ ( _O|I, α_ ) are the category predictions of pre-trained detectors (Liu et al., 2021). Both _α_ and _β_ performances significantly decline, as explained by Fig. 7 and 8. **(2) Losses.** The performances slightly drop after removing category-level _LAi, LBi_ , but significantly drop without instance-level _Lα, Lβ_ . **(3) Feature dimension.** For _fAi, fBi, fα, fβ_ , smaller and larger feature sizes all have degrading effects. **(4) TDE-related implementations.** We probe some different methods: (a) Mean aggregation: _fα[′]_[=][ �] _i[f][α] p_[; (b) Max-pooling aggregation:] _[f] α[ ′]_[is the max value of] _[ f][α] p_ as each component; (c) Random counterfactual feature: assigned random vector as the counterfactual attribute feature (instead of zero vector) during TDE. These methods perform worse than the chosen setting on TDE performance but are comparable on _α_ and _β_ performance.

**Discussion** . Overall, OCL poses extreme challenges to current AI systems. It expects representative learning to accurately recognize attribute and affordance from raw data meanwhile causal inference to capture the causalities within diverse instances and contexts, i.e., both the _intuitive System 1 and logical System 2_ (Bengio, 2019). From the experiments, we find that models struggle to achieve satisfying results on all tracks simultaneously. Notably, it is difficult to achieve a satisfying TDE score via data fitting, and there is much room for improvement. For future studies, a harmonious performance on _α, β_ , and causality learning are encouraged to capture object knowledge better. Potential directions may include _causal representation learning_ (Sch¨olkopf et al., 2021), _casual reinforcement learning_ (Bareinboim, 2020), _neural-symbolic reasoning_ (Besold et al., 2017), etc.

## 6 CONCLUSION

In this work, we introduce object concept learning (OCL), which expects machines to infer affordances and explain what attributes enable an object to possess these affordances. Accordingly, we build an extensive real-world dataset and present a baseline OCRN based on casual intervention and instantiation. OCRN achieves decent performance and follows the causalities well. However, OCL remains challenging and would inspire a line of studies upon reasoning-based object understanding.

## 7 ETHICS STATEMENT

This work primarily proposes a daily object dataset to advance machine understanding of objects for the future study of embodied AI, intelligent robots, etc. Our work may help the home service robots better understand the human living scenes and then help to improve our lives, especially for the health care of patients, elders, and children. All the data used in OCL are publicly available. We have cited the creators and followed the official licenses. For all images, we only provide the URL links in our future web for research uses only. Moreover, modern deep learning models are usually computationally expensive. Our proposed model OCRN also needs multiple GPUs to train. In the future, OCL encourages a new line of studies to understand objects’ common sense and combine causal reasoning and deep learning. We believe the future works focusing on learning the causality instead of heavily fitting data would be more computationally lite, less data-hungry, and low-carbon.

## 8 REPRODUCIBILITY STATEMENT

We provide our source code, OCL dataset samples, and a video demo in the supplementary material (the zip file). It includes all the manuscripts to preprocess and load data, construct the OCRN model with the hyper-parameters, train and test the model, evaluate the performance on the OCL benchmark, etc. Please refer to the code file for more details. The details of related datasets, OCL data processing and annotation are in the main text Sec. 3, Appendix Sec. A.3, A.11. Our code and data will be publicly available.

The paper proposes an ambitious and timely benchmark for object concept learning that integrates category, attribute, and affordance levels with causal reasoning—a valuable direction for embodied AI. It makes a strong case for moving beyond recognition by introducing structured annotations and a reasoning-oriented evaluation. The causal intervention framework (OCRN) is conceptually well-motivated and aligns with recent trends in neurocausal modeling. However, several foundational issues undermine the validity and utility of both the benchmark and the method.

Overview issues:

- Insufficient justification for causal graph structure: The paper assumes a fixed causal graph (Fig. 4) where object category determines attributes and affordances, and attributes cause affordances. However, no empirical or theoretical justification is provided for this specific causal ordering. In reality, context and affordances can influence perceived attributes (e.g., a knife may appear sharp because it is used for cutting), and categories themselves may be inferred from affordances. The model's performance on TDE depends critically on this assumed graph, yet alternative structures are not considered. Without sensitivity analysis or validation of the causal assumptions—such as through human studies or counterfactual probes—the risk of encoding spurious dependencies remains high, especially given the potential for annotation bias in the dataset.

- Ambiguous and potentially circular definition of causal relations: The causal relations between instance-level attributes (α) and affordances (β) are annotated based on 'direct cause' judgments, but the criteria for what constitutes a direct cause are not specified. For example, is 'sharp' a direct cause of 'cut', or is it mediated by 'metal' or 'rigid'? The annotation protocol relies on states and expert consensus, but no inter-annotator agreement metrics are reported. Moreover, since both α and β are manually labeled, the causal labels may reflect annotator intuition rather than ground-truth mechanisms. This risks circularity: the model is trained and evaluated on causal labels that are themselves derived from the same human judgments the model is supposed to replicate, weakening the claim of true causal reasoning.

- Overstated generalization claims due to dataset construction artifacts: The paper claims the benchmark supports generalization to unseen categories and combinations, but the construction process undermines this. Attributes and affordances were selected from existing datasets (e.g., Chao et al. 2015, Zhu et al. 2014), which limits novelty and may propagate biases. More critically, instance-level affordance β is assigned based on predefined states (1,376 total), which are derived from expert consensus rather than observed in images. This means β is not inferred from visual input but from a symbolic state label, making the task more of a lookup than perception-based reasoning. As a result, models may learn to map states to affordances without engaging with visual features, limiting real-world applicability.

- Evaluation metrics do not disentangle recognition from reasoning: The mAP for α and β conflates visual recognition with conceptual understanding, while the TDE metric depends on the quality of the causal annotations. The paper lacks a clear separation between these components. For instance, a model could achieve high mAP by memorizing co-occurrence patterns without understanding causality, yet still show non-zero TDE due to annotation artifacts. Conversely, a model with good causal structure but poor visual features might score low on mAP but high on TDE. The current evaluation does not isolate these dimensions, making it difficult to interpret whether improvements are due to better reasoning or better recognition. A diagnostic test—such as evaluating on out-of-distribution attribute-affordance pairs—would strengthen the claims.

- Inadequate ablation of core causal mechanism: The ablation study in Table 3 removes 'deconfounding' by replacing the prior P(O_i) with P(O_i|I), but this does not fully isolate the effect of causal intervention. The version 'w/o deconfounding' still uses category-level features f_{A_i} and f_{B_i}, meaning the model retains access to category-attribute and category-affordance associations. A stronger ablation would remove the use of category-level knowledge entirely (i.e., no f_{A_i}, f_{B_i}) to test whether OCRN’s performance relies on the hierarchical instantiation mechanism. Without this, the contribution of causal intervention versus simple multi-level supervision remains unclear.

- Lack of comparison to modern vision-language models: The baselines include older or simplified architectures (e.g., DM-V, LingCorr), but omit recent vision-language models like CLIP, LLaVA, or Flamingo that are explicitly designed for open-vocabulary reasoning and attribute-affordance learning. Given that OCL uses human-annotated labels and structured knowledge, a strong foundation model could potentially outperform OCRN by leveraging pre-trained semantic priors. The absence of such comparisons weakens the paper's claim of advancement. Including even one such baseline would better contextualize OCRN's performance and highlight whether the proposed causal framework provides gains beyond what scale and pretraining already offer.

- No test of generalization to unseen attribute-affordance combinations: The paper claims the OCL task supports generalization to novel combinations of attributes and affordances, but no experiment evaluates this. A standard way to validate compositional generalization in concept learning is to hold out certain attribute-affordance pairs during training and test the model’s ability to infer them when they appear in new objects or contexts. For example, if 'sharp → cut' is never observed for knives in the training set, can the model still infer it at test time? Without such a split, the results may reflect memorization of frequent co-occurrences rather than true reasoning. To address this, the authors should create a compositional generalization split—e.g., by excluding all instances where a specific [α_p, β_q] pair occurs—and report performance on these held-out combinations, comparing OCRN to baselines.

- Missing human validation of learned concepts: The paper introduces causal annotations based on expert judgments but does not validate whether the model's predictions align with human expectations. In concept learning, it is essential to show that the system captures human-like understanding. A natural evaluation would involve human subjects judging whether the model’s inferred affordances and their attributed causes (via TDE) are plausible. For instance, given an image and the model’s top-ranked causal attribute for an affordance, humans could rate the reasonableness on a Likert scale. This would ground the TDE metric in human cognition and support the claim that OCRN learns meaningful concepts. Without such a study, the 'reasoning' remains a technical artifact of the training setup.

- No analysis of failure modes in causal reasoning: The paper reports average TDE and mAP scores but does not examine where or why the model fails in causal reasoning. For example, are certain types of attributes (e.g., material vs. shape) more likely to be correctly linked to affordances? Are there systematic errors in rare categories or underrepresented state transitions? A breakdown of TDE performance by attribute and affordance type—especially for negative or counterintuitive cases (e.g., 'broken → cannot eat')—would reveal the limits of the approach. The authors should compute TDE scores stratified by attribute category (e.g., physical, visual, functional) and report per-pair performance to identify structural weaknesses in the model or dataset.

- Underspecified implementation of causal intervention in training: The paper describes using causal intervention via backdoor adjustment but does not clarify how this is implemented in practice during training. Specifically, Eq. 3 replaces P(O|I) with P(O), but it is unclear whether this is done using the empirical prior or through a reweighting scheme. Moreover, the model still uses category predictions from a detector in the non-deconfounded ablation, raising questions about how cleanly the intervention is isolated. To ensure reproducibility and clarify the mechanism, the authors should provide pseudocode or a diagram showing exactly how the intervention is applied in the forward pass—especially whether category-level features are detached or masked during training. This is critical for others to build on the method.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Justify the assumed causal graph with human validation or sensitivity analysis over alternative structures.

- Clarify and validate the causal annotation protocol, including reporting inter-annotator agreement and providing examples of ambiguous cases.

- Revise the evaluation to separate visual recognition from causal reasoning, possibly through controlled generalization tests.

- Include comparisons to modern vision-language foundation models as baselines.

- Provide a more rigorous ablation of the causal intervention mechanism by testing a version without access to category-level attributes and affordances.

comments.title: Ambiguous causal relation labeling logic
comments.quote: Finally, we obtain about 2 M instance- $\alpha$ - $\beta$  triplets of causal relations. As we have labelled all  $\alpha$  and  $\beta$  for all instances, the causal relations would be in four situations: [0,0], [1,1]; [0,1], [1,0]. The former two are "positive", e.g., fresh(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  eat(1/0) for an apple. While the last two are "negative", e.g., broken(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  drive(0/1) for a car.
comments.explanation: The text labels co-occurrence patterns like [0,0] (both absent) as "positive" causal relations, but absence of both attribute and affordance cannot indicate a direct causal link—it may reflect coincidence. For example, a non-broken cup (broken=0) that cannot be driven (drive=0) does not imply a positive causal relationship. The example for the "negative" case is also ambiguous: "drive(0/1)" does not clarify whether the numbers refer to presence or reversed causality. This undermines the validity of the causal labels. The authors should explicitly define causal directionality—e.g., broken → ¬drive—and explain how annotators distinguish direct causation from correlation in such cases.

comments.title: TDE score definition conflates correctness with causal influence
comments.quote:
also expected. We set TDE score $TDE^{\alpha_{p}}_{\beta_{q}}$ as:

\[ TDE^{\alpha_{p}}_{\beta_{q}}=\begin{cases}max(P(\beta_{q})-P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q}),0),>_{\beta_{q}}=1,\\
max(P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q})-P(\beta_{q}),0),>_{\beta_{q}}=0,\end{cases} \] (8)

where $GT_{\beta_{q}}$ is the label of $\beta_{q}$.

(2) $\alpha$-
comments.explanation: The TDE score uses the ground-truth label $GT_{\beta_q}$ to determine the direction of the expected change, but this conflates model correctness with causal influence. A model could incorrectly assign high $P(\beta_q)$ due to a spurious correlation, yet still receive a high TDE score when masking $\alpha_p$, simply because $GT_{\beta_q}=1$. The metric should instead condition on the annotated causal direction—e.g., whether $\alpha_p \to \beta_q$ or $\alpha_p \to \neg\beta_q$—independent of the label. Otherwise, the score rewards models that are correct for the wrong reasons, undermining its validity as a causal reasoning measure.

comments.title: TDE loss misaligned with causal structure
comments.explanation: The TDE loss conditions on $GT_{\beta_q}$ rather than the causal annotation, meaning it encourages large differences in $P(\beta_q)$ when $\beta_q$ is present, regardless of whether $\alpha_p$ is a true cause. This risks reinforcing spurious correlations—e.g., a frequently co-occurring but non-causal attribute—simply because $\beta_q$ is often true. The loss should instead depend on whether $\alpha_p \to \beta_q$ is annotated, not on the label. Otherwise, the model learns to align with label statistics rather than causal mechanisms, weakening the claim of causal reasoning.

comments.title: Zero-masking may not isolate causal effect
comments.quote: we assign zero-mask *(Tang et al., 2020b)* to the feature of $\alpha_{p}$ and keep the other attribute features, to get $P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q})$ with the same model parameters.
comments.explanation: Zero-masking the feature of $\alpha_p$ assumes that this operation cleanly removes only its influence. However, if attribute features are correlated (e.g., 'sharp' and 'metal'), zeroing one may distort the representation and indirectly affect others. This violates the assumption of independent interventions required for valid causal effect estimation. The method risks measuring artifacts of feature corruption rather than true causal effects. While computationally convenient, the authors should acknowledge this limitation and clarify that the intervention is an approximation under feature independence.

comments.title: Inconsistent ablation results in Table 3
comments.explanation: The 'w/o deconfounding' variant achieves a higher α-β-TDE (18.1) than the full OCRN model (17.0), despite lower TDE (18.2 vs 20.5) and lower α/β mAP. Since α-β-TDE is defined as TDE weighted by prediction accuracy, OCRN should dominate on both components. The inconsistency suggests either a calculation error or a mismatch between the reported metric and its stated definition. The authors should verify and correct the values to ensure internal consistency.

comments.title: Causal graph contradicts model input structure
comments.quote: As  $I$  indicates the physical noumenon, it is the source of semantic and functional properties and decides  $\alpha, \beta$ . As attr
comments.explanation: The causal graph implies $I \rightarrow \alpha$, but the model uses $f_{A_i}$—a function of $O_i$—as input to $\mathcal{F}_\alpha$, introducing a backdoor path $I \leftarrow O_i \rightarrow A_i \rightarrow \alpha$. This violates the stated causal structure and undermines the deconfounding argument, as $f_{A_i}$ reintroduces category bias even if $P(O_i|I)$ is replaced with $P(O_i)$. The authors should revise the graph or clarify that $\alpha$ depends on $I$, $O$, and $A$ jointly.

comments.title: Incorrect causal intervention derivation
comments.explanation: The second equality assumes $P(\alpha | I, A_j) = P(\alpha | I, O_j)$, but $\alpha$ depends on $A_j$ through $\mathcal{F}_\alpha$, which may not be independent of $O_i$ given $A_j$. The simplification skips a critical dependency and incorrectly collapses the sum. The derivation should directly use $P(\alpha | I, O_i) = \sum_j P(\alpha | I, A_j) P(A_j | O_i)$ without reindexing, to avoid invalid assumptions about conditional independence.

comments.title: Ambiguous feature mapping for category-level attributes
comments.quote: we represent nodes  $\{I, A, B, \alpha, \beta\}$  as  $\{f_I, f_A, f_B, f_\alpha, f_\beta\}$  respectively in latent space.
comments.explanation: While $f_I$ is defined as RoI-pooled ResNet features, there is no explanation of how $f_A$ or $f_B$ are constructed. Are they learned embeddings, one-hot vectors, or derived from text? This is critical because $f_{A_i}$ is used in $\mathcal{F}_\alpha$. The authors should specify how $f_{A_i}$ is obtained—e.g., via an embedding layer or mean visual prototype—to ensure reproducibility.

comments.title: Misleading claim about preventing data fitting
comments.quote: Besides, we consider and annotate the causal relations between three levels to evaluate the reasoning ability of models and keep the follow-up methods from fitting data only.
comments.explanation: The claim that causal annotations prevent models from 'fitting data only' is unsupported. The labels are derived from expert consensus without validation, and nothing in the training setup prevents memorization of (α → β) co-occurrence patterns. True prevention of data fitting requires architectural or evaluation design (e.g., out-of-distribution splits), not annotation alone. The authors should rephrase to clarify that causal labels enable evaluation of reasoning, not enforcement of it during training.

comments.title: Incorrect attribution of affordance concept
comments.quote: The concept of affordance is introduced by Gibson (2014).
comments.explanation: This is factually incorrect. James J. Gibson introduced the concept of affordance in the 1970s, not 2014. The 2014 reference is likely a reprint or secondary source. Attributing the *introduction* of the concept to that date misrepresents its historical development. The authors should correct the citation to reflect Gibson's original work.

comments.title: Overstated environmental benefits of causal models
comments.quote: We believe the future works focusing on learning the causality instead of heavily fitting data would be more computationally lite, less data-hungry, and low-carbon.
comments.explanation: This claim is speculative and contradicted by the paper's own description: OCRN requires 'multiple GPUs to train'. While causal reasoning can improve generalization, there is no evidence that it universally reduces computational cost or data needs. The authors should revise to reflect uncertainty and avoid broad generalizations about efficiency.

comments.title: Typographical error in key methodological term
comments.quote: present a baseline OCRN based on casual intervention and instantiation.
comments.explanation: The term 'casual intervention' is a clear typo; the intended term is 'causal intervention', which is central to the paper's methodology. This error appears in a key claim about the model's foundation and undermines technical credibility. It should be corrected immediately.

comments.title: Inconsistent state-based affordance assignment
comments.quote: Next,  $\beta$  is annotated for each state and the instances are assigned with beta according to their states. More details please refer to Appendix Sec. A.2. It is worth noting that, as states seem to be a beneficial latent variable for OCL, in practice, we do not use it in models because the states we use are a subset of whole state space, and there can be unseen states in real-world data.
comments.explanation: If $\beta$ is derived entirely from expert-defined states during annotation, then removing access to states at training time does not eliminate their influence—the affordance labels themselves are already state-contingent. The model indirectly learns from states through $\beta$, undermining the claim that it operates without them. The authors should clarify that states guide labeling but are not provided as input to any model.

coarse__glm-4.6.label: Coarse (glm-4.6)
coarse__glm-4.6.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces Object Concept Learning (OCL), a new task requiring models to jointly infer object attributes and affordances while respecting their causal relationships. The authors construct a large-scale dataset with dense annotations across three levels (category, attribute, affordance) and propose OCRN, a baseline model using causal intervention. While the task is well-motivated and the dataset construction appears thorough, there are significant concerns about the evaluation of causal reasoning, the validity of the causal assumptions, and the generalizability of the approach.

The paper makes a valuable contribution by formalizing object concept learning and providing a richly annotated dataset that goes beyond traditional recognition tasks. The integration of causal reasoning with deep learning for object understanding is timely and important. The OCRN baseline shows promising results on attribute and affordance prediction, and the ablation studies provide useful insights. However, the evaluation of causal learning appears problematic, and some methodological choices need stronger justification.

Overview issues:

- TDE Evaluation Does Not Properly Measure Causal Understanding: The Total Direct Effect (TDE) evaluation in Section 5 has a fundamental flaw: it measures whether the model's affordance prediction changes when an attribute feature is masked, but this does not verify whether the model has learned the correct causal mechanism. The method assumes that zero-masking the feature simulates removing the attribute's causal effect, which is not valid - the model may simply be responding to feature removal rather than demonstrating causal reasoning. This is evident in Table 2 where even simple baselines like DM-At achieve non-zero TDE scores. The random counterfactual ablation (Table 3) showing near-zero TDE scores further suggests the metric is sensitive to implementation details rather than causal understanding. A proper evaluation would require testing whether the model correctly predicts counterfactual outcomes under well-defined interventions, not just feature ablations.

- Causal Graph Assumptions Lack Justification: The causal graph in Figure 4 makes strong assumptions about the relationships between object category, attributes, and affordances that are not empirically validated. The paper assumes attributes always causally influence affordances (edges A→B and α→β), but this is context-dependent. For example, 'red' (attribute) does not cause 'edible' (affordance) for a apple - both are caused by the object's ripeness state. The annotation process (Section 3) reduces complex causal relationships to binary decisions, potentially oversimplifying reality. The paper should provide evidence that annotators can reliably identify direct causes versus mere correlations, and include analysis of annotation consistency for causal relations. Without this validation, the learned causal structure may reflect annotator biases rather than true causal mechanisms.

- Deconfounding Implementation Is Approximate: The claimed deconfounding through causal intervention (Equation 3) is implemented approximately using category priors and mean features. The intervention P(α|do(I)) is approximated by averaging over all categories weighted by their prior probabilities, which does not truly eliminate the confounding effect of the object category. The visual appearance I still contains category information that influences the attribute prediction. The ablation study (Table 3) shows only modest improvements from deconfounding (TDE: 20.5 vs 18.2), suggesting the effect is limited. A more rigorous approach would require explicit disentanglement of category and attribute features or stronger interventions that truly break the category-appearance dependency.

- Benchmark May Not Capture True Concept Learning: Despite the paper's claims, the OCL benchmark may not adequately measure 'concept learning' beyond sophisticated pattern matching. The tasks are formulated as multi-label classification problems with additional constraints, and the OCRN architecture is essentially a carefully designed multi-task network. The impressive performance on β (affordance) compared to α (attribute) in Table 2 suggests affordances may be more correlated with visual features than the authors acknowledge. The paper should include experiments demonstrating that models cannot achieve high performance by exploiting dataset biases or spurious correlations, such as training with shuffled causal labels or using category-only baselines. Without such controls, it's unclear whether the benchmark truly requires causal reasoning or can be solved by advanced feature learning.

- Limited Analysis of Generalization and Failure Cases: The paper lacks thorough analysis of how OCRN generalizes to unseen objects, attributes, or affordances - a key aspect of concept learning. While the dataset covers 381 categories, the long-tail distribution (rarest categories with only 9 instances) raises concerns about generalization to rare objects. Section 5 mentions applying OCRN to Human-Object Interaction Detection but provides no quantitative results on zero-shot or few-shot scenarios. The paper should include experiments testing generalization: (1) performance on held-out categories, (2) ability to infer affordances for novel attribute combinations, and (3) analysis of systematic failure patterns. Without these, it's difficult to assess whether the approach truly learns generalizable object concepts or merely fits the training distribution.

- No concrete example of causal reasoning: The paper introduces the OCL task and OCRN method but never walks through a complete example showing how the model processes a specific image to reach an affordance decision with causal justification. For instance, it would be valuable to trace through how OCRN handles a 'broken cup' image: showing the predicted attributes, how the intervention affects affordance predictions, and demonstrating that the model correctly suppresses 'hold water' while maintaining other affordances. This worked example would help readers understand the instantiation mechanism and verify that the causal intervention works as intended.

- Missing inference for novel compositions: The paper claims OCL enables concept learning but provides no evidence that models can generalize to novel attribute-affordance combinations not seen during training. A crucial test would be to hold out specific [attribute, affordance] pairs and evaluate whether OCRN can still infer the correct relationship using causal reasoning. For example, if 'transparent'→'see-through' is held out, can the model still infer this relationship for a 'transparent bottle'? Without such compositional generalization tests, it's unclear whether the approach truly learns concepts or just memorizes patterns.

- No comparison to pure causal baselines: The paper compares OCRN to various vision and language baselines but never against methods that explicitly use causal inference without the complex instantiation mechanism. A natural baseline would be a simple causal graphical model (e.g., a Bayesian network) using the same category, attribute, and affordance labels. This would isolate the contribution of the neurocausal architecture from the value of causal reasoning itself. The absence of such a comparison makes it difficult to assess whether OCRN's performance comes from its causal approach or its neural architecture.

- Insufficient implementation guidance: While the paper provides architectural details, it lacks practical guidance for researchers to implement the causal intervention correctly. The description of how to compute P(O_i) priors, handle the long-tail distribution during instantiation, and implement the TDE evaluation is incomplete. For reproducibility, the paper should specify: (1) how to compute category priors for rare categories, (2) whether the mean category features are updated during training, and (3) the exact procedure for generating counterfactuals during TDE evaluation beyond 'zero-masking'. Without these details, reproducing the claimed causal benefits is challenging.

Recommendation: major revision

Revision targets:

- Redesign the TDE evaluation to properly assess causal understanding, potentially using counterfactual queries with explicit interventions rather than feature masking.

- Provide empirical validation of the causal graph assumptions through annotation consistency studies and analysis of alternative causal structures.

- Implement a more rigorous deconfounding approach that better separates category and attribute information, with ablation studies showing its necessity.

- Add controlled experiments demonstrating that the benchmark cannot be solved without causal reasoning (e.g., with shuffled labels or category-only baselines).

- Include comprehensive generalization tests on unseen objects/attributes and detailed failure case analysis to demonstrate true concept learning.

comments.title: TDE loss contradicts evaluation metric
comments.quote:
We adopt an extra Hinge loss to maximize the TDE score of all  $[\alpha_p, \beta_q]$ . In detail, without the effect of one certain  $\alpha_p$ , we intend the probability of causal-related  $\beta_q$  to keep farther from  $GT_{\beta_q}$  by a margin  $\tau$  ( $= 0.1$  in experiments), formulated as:

$$
TDE^{\alpha_p}_{\beta_q} = \left\{ \begin{array}{l l} \max \{0, \tau - (P(\beta_q) - P^{\alpha_p}_{TDE}(\beta_q)) \}, & GT_{\beta_q} = 1, \\ \max \{0, \tau - (P^{\alpha_p}_{TDE}(\beta_q) - P(\beta_q)) \}, & GT_{\beta_q} = 0. \end{array} \right. \tag{9}
$$
comments.explanation: The TDE loss function directly contradicts the evaluation metric. Equation 8 rewards large differences between original and counterfactual predictions for causal pairs, but Equation 9 penalizes differences larger than τ by making the loss zero when the gap exceeds τ. This means the model has no incentive to maximize the causal effect beyond a small margin, which undermines the entire evaluation premise. The loss should encourage large differences for causal pairs and small differences for non-causal pairs, not constrain causal effects to a tiny margin.

comments.title: Incorrect causal intervention derivation
comments.quote:
8).

Causal Intervention. To tackle this problem, we propose OCRN utilizing intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) to deconfound the confounder  $O$  for  $\alpha$  (Fig. 5). That said, in  $\alpha$  estimation, we use  $do(\cdot)$  operation (Pearl et al., 2016) to eliminate the edge from  $O$  to  $I$ , i.e.,  $P(\alpha | do(I))$  is

$$
\sum_{i}^{m} P(\alpha|I, O_i) P(O_i) = \sum_{i}^{m} P(O_i) \sum_{j}^{m} P(\alpha|I, A_j) P(A_j|O_i) = \sum_{i}^{m} P(\alpha|I, A_i) P(O_i), \tag{3}
$$

<!-- PAGE BREAK -
comments.explanation: The derivation contains a mathematical error in the second equality. The step from P(α|I,O_i) to ∑_j P(α|I,A_j) P(A_j|O_i) assumes α is directly determined by category-level attributes A_j, but the causal graph shows α is instantiated from A through a separate mechanism. This conflation of category-level and instance-level variables invalidates the derivation. The correct approach should acknowledge that P(α|I,O_i) cannot be expanded this way without additional assumptions about the instantiation process.

comments.title: TDE evaluation formula has rendering error
comments.quote:
We set TDE score $TDE^{\alpha_{p}}_{\beta_{q}}$ as:

\[ TDE^{\alpha_{p}}_{\beta_{q}}=\begin{cases}max(P(\beta_{q})-P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q}),0),>_{\beta_{q}}=1,\\
max(P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q})-P(\beta_{q}),0),>_{\beta_{q}}=0,\end{cases} \] (8)
comments.explanation: The TDE scoring formula contains LaTeX rendering errors. The case conditions use '>' symbols instead of '=' symbols, making the formula mathematically incorrect. It should read 'GT_{β_q} = 1' and 'GT_{β_q} = 0' rather than '>_{β_q} = 1' and '>_{β_q} = 0'. This appears to be a typesetting error where equality signs were replaced with greater-than symbols.

comments.title: Inconsistent causal relation notation
comments.quote: Finally, we obtain about 2 M instance- $\alpha$ - $\beta$  triplets of causal relations. As we have labelled all  $\alpha$  and  $\beta$  for all instances, the causal relations would be in four situations: [0,0], [1,1]; [0,1], [1,0]. The former two are "positive", e.g., fresh(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  eat(1/0) for an apple. While the last two are "negative", e.g., broken(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  drive(0/1) for a car.
comments.explanation: The notation for causal relations is inconsistent and contradictory. The text defines four situations using [x,y] notation, but the examples use (x/y) notation. More critically, the example 'fresh(1/0) → eat(1/0)' is labeled as 'positive' but contains both (1,0) and (0,1) cases, which were just defined as 'negative' situations. This creates a logical contradiction in the classification scheme. The entire causal relation notation section needs rewriting with consistent notation and examples that match the defined categories.

comments.title: Attention formula has dimensional error
comments.quote: The attention output is $\text{softmax}([Q, Q']^T[K, K'] / \sqrt{d})[V, V']^T$ where $d$ is the feature dimension.
comments.explanation: The attention formula is mathematically incorrect due to dimensional mismatch. If Q and Q' are each d×1 vectors, then [Q,Q']^T[K,K'] produces a scalar, not a matrix suitable for softmax. The correct formulation should use proper matrix multiplication, either by transposing the key matrix or using separate attention computations. Additionally, since [Q,Q'] and [K,K'] are concatenated vectors of length 2d, the scaling factor should be √(2d) rather than √d.

comments.title: Unexplained filtering methodology for causal pairs
comments.quote: Initially, we need to make binary decisions on all instance- $\alpha$ - $\beta$  triplets, which is far beyond handleable. Fortunately, we find that most  $\alpha$ - $\beta$  classes (e.g., shiny and kick) are meaningless and always of no causality on any instances. Thus, we can firstly exclude the most impossible pairs, meanwhile, guarantee the completeness of causality. Finally, we obtain about  $10\%$ $\alpha$ - $\beta$  classes as candidates.
comments.explanation: The filtering process lacks critical methodological details. The paper claims to exclude 'the most impossible pairs' and reduce to 10% while 'guaranteeing completeness,' but provides no explanation of how 'impossible' was determined or how completeness was verified. Without specifying the filtering criteria (semantic rules, automated metrics, manual inspection) and providing evidence that no meaningful causal relationships were lost, readers cannot assess whether important causal connections were inadvertently excluded.

comments.title: Ambiguous state-based affordance annotation process
comments.quote: As the instances in the same state should have similar  $\beta$  (all rotten apples cannot be eaten), six experts first conclude the states. In total, 1,376 states are defined, and each category has 3.6 states on average. Next,  $\beta$  is annotated for each state and the instances are assigned with beta according to their states.
comments.explanation: The annotation methodology appears circular. The paper states that affordances (β) are annotated 'for each state' and then instances are assigned β 'according to their states,' but never explains how instances are mapped to states in the first place. This creates a dependency loop: you need to know an instance's state to assign its affordance, but you need to know its affordance to determine its state. Clarify whether states were determined independently (e.g., by visual inspection) or through an iterative process.

comments.title: Missing justification for intervention formula
comments.quote: To tackle this problem, we propose OCRN utilizing intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) to deconfound the confounder  $O$  for  $\alpha$  (Fig. 5). That said, in  $\alpha$  estimation, we use  $do(\cdot)$  operation (Pearl et al., 2016) to eliminate the edge from  $O$  to  $I$ , i.e.,  $P(\alpha | do(I))$  is
comments.explanation: The paper applies the do-operator to eliminate the O→I edge but doesn't justify why this specific intervention is sufficient for deconfounding. According to Pearl's causal calculus, applying do(I) requires specifying the intervention mechanism and ensuring all backdoor paths are blocked. The paper doesn't explain whether there are other confounding paths or why eliminating only the O→I edge addresses category bias. The causal graph shows multiple paths, and the intervention's effect needs formal justification using backdoor or front-door criteria.

comments.title: Undefined instantiation functions
comments.quote: Attribute and affordance modules are the **instantiations** of category-level features. Mean object category feature $f_{O_i}$ is converted to $f_{A_i}$ or $f_{B_i}$ by an FC, and then instantiated via $\mathcal{F}_{\alpha}(f_I, f_{A_i})$ or $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}(f_I, f_{\alpha}, f_{B_i})$.
comments.explanation: The instantiation functions F_α and F_β are crucial for the causal intervention derivation but never defined mathematically. Without specifying their form or properties, the derivation in Equation 3 is incomplete—it assumes a specific relationship between P(α|I,A_i) and the instantiation process that is never justified. The paper should either define these functions explicitly or state the assumptions made about their behavior in the causal derivation.

comments.title: Inconsistent causal hierarchy description
comments.quote:
If the category is the first level of object concept, the attribute can be seen as the second level closer to the physical fact.

However, recognizing attributes is still far away from concept understanding. Given a hammer, we need to know it can be held to hit nails. That is, requiring machines to infer affordance *(Gibson, 2014)* indicating what actions humans can perform upon objects. Here, we refer to affordance as the third level, which is closely related to common sense and causal inference *(Gibson, 2014)*.
comments.explanation: The paper presents an inconsistent causal hierarchy. It first states attributes are 'closer to the physical fact' than categories, suggesting attributes are more fundamental. But then it claims affordances are the 'third level' above attributes, which contradicts Gibson's original formulation where affordances are directly perceived properties. The entire OCRN architecture depends on this causal ordering, so the paper must clarify whether affordances are derived from attributes or directly perceived from objects.

comments.title: Exaggerated deconfounding performance claim
comments.quote: Both  $\alpha$  and  $\beta$  performances significantly decline, as explained by Fig. 7 and 8.
comments.explanation: The claim of 'significant decline' in α and β performance when removing deconfounding is not supported by Table 3. The α performance drops from 32.4 to 32.1 (only 0.3 mAP), and β drops from 52.2 to 51.8 (only 0.4 mAP). These are minimal declines, not significant ones. More importantly, the α-β-TDE score actually increases slightly from 17.0 to 18.1 when deconfounding is removed, contradicting the claim that deconfounding helps overall performance.

comments.title: Notation inconsistency in mean feature
comments.quote: We take the mean feature $(\bar{f}_{O_i})$ of all instances in one category as the representation of the category $(O_i)$. It is then mapped to attribute feature space: $f_{A_i} = \mathcal{F}_A(f_{O_i})$, where $\mathcal{F}_A(\cdot)$ is a fully-connected layer (FC) (Fig. 5).
comments.explanation: The text explicitly defines the mean feature as (\bar{f}_{O_i}) but the subsequent equation uses f_{O_i} instead of \bar{f}_{O_i}. This creates ambiguity about whether the mean feature or the original feature is being mapped. Rewrite the equation as f_{A_i} = \mathcal{F}_A(\bar{f}_{O_i}) to maintain consistency with the description.

comments.title: Undefined prior probability notation
comments.quote:
q. 3:

$$
f_{\alpha} = \sum_{i}^{m} \mathcal{F}_{\alpha}(f_I, f_{A_i}) P_{O_i}, \tag{4}
$$
where $P_{O_i}$ is the prior category probability ($P(O_i)$ in Eq. 3). Eq.
comments.explanation: The paper references Eq. 3 for the definition of P_{O_i}, but this creates a dependency that makes Eq. 4 difficult to understand in isolation. Add a brief explanation of how P_{O_i} is obtained (e.g., from training data statistics) to make the implementation of Eq. 4 self-contained.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated causal performance claim
comments.quote: OCRN achieves decent performance and follows the causalities well.
comments.explanation: The claim that OCRN 'follows the causalities well' is not substantiated by the experiments. The paper's own analysis shows the TDE evaluation has fundamental flaws, and Table 3 demonstrates that even simple baselines achieve non-zero TDE scores. The random counterfactual ablation showing near-zero TDE scores suggests the metric is sensitive to implementation details rather than genuine causal reasoning. Without properly designed causal interventions, this claim overstates what the experiments demonstrate.

coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.label: Coarse (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
coarse__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a new benchmark, Object Concept Learning (OCL), which aims to move beyond simple object recognition by requiring models to reason about attributes and affordances using causal inference. While the dataset construction is ambitious, the methodology relies on several strong assumptions about the static nature of affordances and the validity of the causal graph.

The paper makes a notable contribution by curating a large, multi-level dataset that links categories, attributes, and affordances. The proposed OCRN baseline attempts to address category bias using causal intervention, which is a promising direction for this field. However, the reliance on static, pre-defined causal relations and the potential for data leakage between the knowledge base and the model's reasoning process need further clarification.

Overview issues:

- Conflation of Correlation and Causality in the Knowledge Base: In Section 3, the authors define causal relations between attributes and affordances based on expert annotation of 'states.' However, the paper treats these as ground-truth causal links without empirical validation of whether these relations hold in diverse, out-of-distribution contexts. If the knowledge base is built on human common sense, it may capture human-labeling biases rather than physical causal realities. The authors should provide an analysis of the inter-annotator agreement specifically for the causal links and discuss how the model handles cases where the annotated 'cause' (e.g., 'heavy') does not actually produce the 'effect' (e.g., 'can hit') in a novel visual setting.

- Potential Data Leakage via Category-Level Priors: Section 4.1 and 4.2 describe using category-level attribute matrices (M_A) and affordance matrices (M_B) as part of the reasoning process. Because these matrices are constructed from the same dataset instances used for training, there is a high risk that the model is simply performing a lookup or associative recall rather than true causal reasoning. The authors must clarify if the category-level priors were constructed using a strictly disjoint subset of data or external knowledge sources. Without this, it is difficult to determine if the performance gains are due to the causal framework or simply the memorization of dataset-specific co-occurrence statistics.

- Simplistic Handling of Context-Dependent Affordances: The methodology assumes that affordances can be inferred primarily through attribute estimation (Section 4.2.2). This ignores the crucial role of spatial configuration and environmental interaction, which are known to be essential for affordance detection. While the authors mention that they do not use human-object spatial configuration due to cost, this omission effectively limits the 'concept learning' to a classification task of visual features. The paper should explicitly acknowledge this limitation and provide an ablation or discussion on how the model performs when the visual appearance of an object is decoupled from its typical context.

- Lack of Robustness Analysis for OOD Inputs: The evaluation in Section 5 relies on mAP metrics on a held-out test set, but it does not test the model's ability to generalize to novel objects or unseen attribute-affordance combinations. Given the claim that the model learns 'concepts,' it should be evaluated on its ability to infer affordances for objects that appear in non-standard states or environments not represented in the training set. Adding a zero-shot or cross-category evaluation track would significantly strengthen the claim that the model is learning concepts rather than just patterns.

- Lack of zero-shot generalization demonstration for novel concept combinations: The paper claims to move beyond simple recognition toward 'concept learning', yet the current evaluation is restricted to a standard train-test split on the same dataset. To support the claim of learning concepts rather than memorizing associations, the authors should include a zero-shot evaluation track. Specifically, they should report performance on 'unseen' attribute-affordance pairs or novel object-attribute combinations not present in the training set. This would demonstrate that the model actually generalizes the learned causal rules to novel scenarios, which is a standard requirement for verifying claims of concept learning.

- Absence of a concrete, fully-specified causal intervention example: The paper discusses causal intervention (Pearl et al., 2016) in the abstract and methodology but lacks a worked example that traces a specific instance through the OCRN pipeline. Readers would benefit from a step-by-step calculation for a single object (e.g., a 'broken cup') showing how the model's prediction of 'hold water' changes when the 'broken' attribute is masked or intervened upon. Providing this concrete computation would clarify how the model's internal causal graph handles conflicting information and would validate the effectiveness of the proposed debiasing mechanism.

- Missing cross-dataset validation to demonstrate robustness: The paper relies entirely on the newly introduced OCL benchmark for evaluation. To prove that the proposed OCRN architecture and causal reasoning framework are not overfitted to the specific biases of the OCL dataset, the authors should evaluate the model on an existing, external dataset such as the 'Zhu et al. (2014)' affordance dataset. Demonstrating that the model trained on OCL can transfer its reasoning capabilities to a different, smaller-scale dataset would provide necessary evidence that the learned concepts are transferable and not merely artifacts of the OCL annotation process.

Recommendation: major revision. The paper proposes a valuable dataset and a coherent baseline, but the claims regarding 'causal reasoning' are currently supported more by the structure of the annotations than by the model's demonstrated ability to generalize. The authors need to address the potential for data leakage and provide more rigorous evidence that the model is not simply exploiting correlations within the training set.

Revision targets:

- Clarify the construction of the category-level matrices (M_A, M_B) to prove they do not introduce data leakage from the test set.

- Perform a zero-shot evaluation on a subset of categories or attribute combinations to demonstrate that the model is learning generalizable concepts.

- Include a sensitivity analysis showing how the model's affordance predictions change when visual features are perturbed, to validate the causal intervention claims.

- Discuss the limitations of the 'static attribute' assumption in the context of real-world robotic interaction.

comments.title: Ambiguity in the definition of the TDE operator
comments.quote: <   P _ {\alpha}, P _ {\beta} > = \mathcal {F} (I, P (O | I)), \Delta P _ {\beta} = T D E [ \mathcal {F} (I, P (O | I)) ], \tag {1}
comments.explanation: The notation for the TDE operator is underspecified. Total Direct Effect (TDE) requires an intervention on a specific variable within a structural causal model, yet the expression here acts on the output of a function $\mathcal{F}$. It is unclear how this operator functions without access to the internal causal graph or a defined intervention target. Please define the intervention target and the counterfactual state used to compute the TDE to ensure the expression is well-defined.

comments.title: Dimensionality mismatch in attention mechanism
comments.quote: The attention output is $\text{softmax}([Q, Q']^T[K, K'] / \sqrt{d})[V, V']^T$ where $d$ is the feature dimension.
comments.explanation: The expression $[Q, Q']^T[K, K']$ results in a scalar, which causes the softmax to output 1, nullifying the attention mechanism. Furthermore, the subsequent multiplication by $[V, V']^T$ results in a vector of dimension $2d$, which contradicts the requirement that the output of $\mathcal{F}_{\alpha}$ must be dimension $d$. Please rewrite the attention output expression to reflect a standard cross-attention operation, ensuring that the dimensions of $Q, K,$ and $V$ are consistent with the input features.

comments.title: Inconsistency in the definition of the input to the affordance instantiation function
comments.quote: $\mathcal{F}_{B}(\cdot),\mathcal{F}_{\beta}(\cdot)$ are implemented the same as $\mathcal{F}_{A}(\cdot),\mathcal{F}_{\alpha}(\cdot)$, except the first input condition of $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}$ is the concatenation of $f^{\prime}_{\alpha},f_{I}$.
comments.explanation: The text states that the first input condition of $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}$ is the concatenation of $f^{\prime}_{\alpha}$ and $f_{I}$, but Equation (7) defines $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}$ as taking three distinct arguments: $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}(f_{I}, f^{\prime}_{\alpha}, f_{B_{i}})$. This ambiguity makes it unclear whether the function expects two or three input tensors. Please clarify the input structure of $\mathcal{F}_{\beta}$ to match the implementation description.

comments.title: Ambiguity in causal filtering methodology
comments.quote: we find that most  $\alpha$ - $\beta$  classes (e.g., shiny and kick) are meaningless and always of no causality on any instances. Thus, we can firstly exclude the most impossible pairs, meanwhile, guarantee the completeness of causality. Finally,
comments.explanation: The claim that you can 'guarantee the completeness of causality' while excluding 90% of potential attribute-affordance pairs is logically unsupported. By definition, excluding pairs based on a heuristic assumes that causality is sparse and that the heuristic does not miss rare but valid causal links. Please provide the specific criteria used for this exclusion or acknowledge that the resulting knowledge base is a subset of potential causal relations rather than a complete one.

comments.title: Inconsistency in causal relation definition
comments.quote: As we have labelled all  $\alpha$  and  $\beta$  for all instances, the causal relations would be in four situations: [0,0], [1,1]; [0,1], [1,0]. The former two are "positive", e.g., fresh(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  eat(1/0) for an apple. While the last two are "negative", e.g., broken(1/0)  $\rightarrow$  drive(0/1) for a car.
comments.explanation: The definition of 'positive' and 'negative' causal relations is ambiguous. The classification of [0,0] as a 'positive' causal relation is problematic, as the absence of both an attribute and an affordance does not constitute a causal link. Furthermore, the 'negative' category [0,1] and [1,0] describes a standard causal inhibitory relationship, not a 'negative' relation in the sense of logical negation. Please clarify whether these categories refer to the sign of the causal influence or the correlation of the binary states.

comments.title: Contradiction between generalization goal and test set coverage
comments.quote: The val and test sets cover 221 of the 381 categories, and the train set covers all categories. OCL is a long-tailed recognition task and requires generalization to cover the whole object category-attribute-affordance space with imbalanced information.
comments.explanation: The authors define the OCL task as requiring generalization over the entire category-attribute-affordance space, yet the benchmark explicitly excludes 160 categories from the validation and test sets. This creates a logical gap where the stated objective cannot be empirically verified. Please clarify that generalization is evaluated only on the subset of 221 categories, or explain how the remaining 160 categories are handled in the evaluation framework.

comments.title: Ambiguity in TDE counterfactual probability
comments.quote: As we use learned attribute feature to infer $\beta_{q}$, we assign zero-mask *(Tang et al., 2020b)* to the feature of $\alpha_{p}$ and keep the other attribute features, to get $P^{\alpha_{p}}_{TDE}(\beta_{q})$ with the same model parameters.
comments.explanation: Zeroing out one component of a concatenated vector may not represent a true counterfactual intervention if the remaining features implicitly encode information about the removed attribute. The authors should clarify if the 'zero-mask' is applied to the input of the affordance module or if it is a structural intervention that accounts for the causal graph.

coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Coarse (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
coarse__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback:
The paper introduces a benchmark for object concept learning and a reasoning network that leverages category prototypes to predict attributes and affordances. The dataset is extensive, and the task definition pushes beyond standard recognition. However, the theoretical framing of causal inference contains fundamental misunderstandings, and the evaluation metrics do not properly test counterfactual reasoning. The annotation process lacks reliability checks, and performance gains over baselines are marginal.

The paper makes a solid contribution by compiling a large-scale dataset with multi-level object concepts and proposing a prototype-based reasoning baseline. The task definition effectively highlights the need for deeper object understanding beyond category recognition. However, the manuscript struggles with theoretical rigor in its causal claims and relies on evaluation metrics that conflate feature ablation with causal intervention. Addressing these foundational issues is necessary to validate the method's novelty and reliability.

Overview issues:

- Theoretical Misalignment with Causal Inference: The paper frames a prior-weighting scheme as causal intervention. Equation 3 replaces the posterior category distribution $P(O_i|I)$ with the training prior $P(O_i)$. In causal inference, intervening on a variable means severing its incoming edges, not ignoring the evidence that determines its value. By marginalizing over categories with fixed priors, the model discards the visual information $I$ provides about the object's identity. This conceptual mismatch weakens the theoretical contribution. The authors should either reframe this as a standard debiasing technique for imbalanced data or formally derive why this specific marginalization satisfies the backdoor criterion for the proposed graph.

- Missing Annotation Reliability Metrics: The dataset construction relies on manually annotated instance-level causal relations between attributes and affordances. The text states that multiple annotators combined their answers, but it omits inter-annotator agreement scores or any measure of consensus. Causal relations in natural scenes are highly subjective and context-dependent. Without reliability metrics, readers cannot gauge the noise level in the ground truth. This omission is critical because the TDE evaluation directly depends on the accuracy of these causal labels. The authors must report Cohen's kappa or similar agreement metrics and provide a detailed error analysis of the annotation process.

- Flawed Causal Evaluation Metric: The TDE metric evaluates causal reasoning by zero-masking a learned attribute feature and measuring the change in affordance probability. This procedure is a standard feature ablation, not a valid causal intervention in a structural causal model. Neural network features are entangled and non-linear; zeroing out a vector does not correspond to setting a variable to a counterfactual value. Furthermore, Equation 8 uses the ground truth label $GT_{ eta_q}$ to dictate the expected direction of the probability change. This makes the metric circular, as it merely checks whether the model's prediction shifts in the direction of the annotation rather than testing genuine counterfactual reasoning. The evaluation should adopt a proper counterfactual framework or clearly reframe the metric as a feature sensitivity analysis.

- Modest Performance and Limited Baselines: The performance improvements of OCRN over baselines are modest, with mAP gains of only 1.0 to 3.1 points and TDE scores remaining below 10 across all methods. The authors claim the model follows causalities well, but the numerical results suggest only slight predictive improvements. Additionally, the baseline selection lacks modern vision-language models or recent causal debiasing approaches. Comparing against only direct mapping, attention mechanisms, and older probabilistic models makes it difficult to assess whether the proposed reasoning mechanism offers a meaningful leap. The revision should include comparisons with contemporary foundation models and clarify whether the marginal gains justify the architectural complexity.

- Model Architecture Contradicts Causal Graph: The causal graph in Figure 4 depicts instance appearance $I$ as the direct cause of instance attributes $ eta$ and affordances $ eta$, with category $O$ operating at a higher level. The model implementation, however, computes instance features by attending over category prototypes weighted by category priors (Equations 4 and 7). This design subordinates the visual evidence $I$ to category-level distributions, contradicting the graph's claim that visual appearance drives instance-level properties. The architecture effectively implements a prototype-based retrieval system rather than the causal instantiation process described.
